lens.included_activities: United States lower-middle-market providers of insurance advisory, insurance actuarial, and insurance ratemaking services performed on a contract or fee basis for insurers, employers, public entities, and other risk-bearing organizations.
lens.excluded_activities: Insurance agencies and brokerages, claims adjusting, third-party plan administration, internal carrier departments, firms outside the United States, shells, and owner-dependent practices that cannot transfer without the seller.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers purchase project work, retainers, recurring valuation or reporting engagements, and specialist analyses. Revenue is generated from professional fees, time-based billing, fixed deliverables, or recurring contracted service scope.

a.bridge: BLS occupational data cover all of NAICS 524290, while BLS describes actuary tasks across employers. Those sources are mapped to the target's data preparation, modeling, documentation, research, reporting, and communication workflows. Broader claims and administrative occupations may overstate routine exposure, while the exact target's analytical work may understate it.
a.rationale: Data extraction, experience studies, model coding, scenario generation, documentation, report drafting, quality checks, and research are materially assistable. Assumption setting, model selection, interpretation, client advice, sign-off, and professional accountability remain human-led.
a.change_evidence: Time studies by engagement type that separate data, modeling, review, communication, and accountable-signatory work would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The occupational evidence is not reported for the exact six-digit code.
Task exposure is not equivalent to job displacement.
The target includes non-actuarial advisory services that may have different task mixes.

rho.bridge: The Society of Actuaries survey covers members rather than firms in NAICS 524298. Reported experimentation and limited practical integration are transferred to actuarial and insurance-advisory practices; member self-selection and the exclusion of non-actuarial advisers may bias adoption in either direction.
rho.rationale: Generative AI, code assistance, document tools, analytics, and optical character recognition can be deployed now, but data privacy, validation, explainability, professional standards, and client restrictions limit realizability. The evidence supports meaningful assistance rather than autonomous end-to-end delivery.
rho.change_evidence: Firm-level production metrics for AI use, review hours, error rates, client acceptance, and realized cycle-time or labor savings would materially change the value.
caveats: Survey awareness and experimentation are not realized productivity.
Professional and regulatory controls can require duplicative human review.
Small samples and member selection limit representativeness.

e.rationale: The exact code is defined around contract or fee insurance advisory, actuarial, and ratemaking services, which aligns well with the lens. Discounts remain necessary for mixed-service establishments, captive or misclassified units, very small owner-dependent practices, and firms outside the financial size band.
e.change_evidence: A verified establishment register with service mix, revenue, ownership, geography, and seller-dependence fields would materially change the eligibility share.
caveats: No direct population audit of qualifying firms was found.
Establishments may not correspond one-for-one with firms.
Residual-code classification can include activities not visible from company descriptions.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures intended sale or transfer among United States employer-business owners across industries. The result is applied with a downward adjustment because professional-services intentions may become internal succession, gradual partner buyouts, or nonqualifying transactions, and key-person dependence can prevent a sale.
s5.rationale: Aging ownership and recurring client books create some control-sale supply, but professional credentials, relationship concentration, partner structures, and seller dependence constrain clean transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A closed-transaction census for actuarial and insurance-advisory firms, including control status and the eligible target denominator, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Owner intentions are not completed control sales.
The proxy is not industry-specific.
Partner admissions, retirements, and book transfers may not qualify.

q.rationale: Recurring valuations, filings, and retainers can preserve some productivity gains, especially under fixed fees and trusted-adviser relationships. Hourly billing, competitive rebids, transparent effort reductions, software costs, and client procurement can pass benefits through, while project work has weaker retention than embedded administration.
q.change_evidence: Engagement-level data connecting billing model, renewal repricing, utilization, software expense, and margins before and after AI deployment would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The official fee-basis definition does not reveal the split between hourly, fixed, and recurring revenue.
No direct evidence of post-AI pricing behavior was found.
Professional review costs may absorb gross productivity gains.

d5.bridge: BLS projects broader insurance-industry output and NAICS 524290 employment, while the actuary outlook covers the occupation across industries. The target is inferred to have modest real growth: actuarial demand may pull upward, while automation, carrier insourcing, and fee pressure may pull downward.
d5.rationale: Risk complexity, regulation, pricing scrutiny, model governance, and demand for quantitative advice support durable services. Standardized analysis can be automated or insourced, limiting revenue growth even when the volume of analysis rises.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-dollar revenue and client-volume histories for the exact contract insurance advisory and actuarial segment would materially change the multiplier.
caveats: Industry and occupation projections answer different questions.
Neither projection isolates the exact six-digit service population.
Professional demand can rise while fee compression limits firm revenue.

o.rationale: Insurers and other risk bearers continue to need defensible models, assumptions, communication, review, and accountable expert judgment. Standard reports and modeling modules can shift to client teams or software platforms, so service demand persists more strongly than any particular incumbent delivery model.
o.change_evidence: Client-level substitution data distinguishing adviser replacement, internalization, software-only delivery, and continued expert engagement would materially change the value.
caveats: Professional standards support accountable work but do not require an external firm.
AI can increase demand for validation while reducing demand for routine production.
No direct substitution study for the exact code was found.

narrative.executive_view: This is a reasonably coherent fee-based insurance analytics and advisory population. It has substantial AI-assistable analytical and documentation work, but professional judgment, validation, communication, and accountability keep realizability and operator survival below raw task exposure.
narrative.ai_impact: The clearest uses are data preparation, research, code assistance, model documentation, scenario generation, report drafting, and quality-control support. Practical integration remains constrained by confidential data, hallucination and model risk, explainability, validation, and professional standards.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed-fee and recurring engagements can retain labor savings, while hourly billing and competitive renewal processes pass more value to clients. Strong client trust and specialist knowledge help, but software and review costs claim part of the benefit.
narrative.firm_availability: The official definition aligns with the lens, yet the true eligible population remains uncertain because firm size, ownership, mixed services, and seller dependence are not observed. Broad succession evidence supports some supply but does not establish an industry transaction rate.
narrative.demand_durability: Insurance pricing, reserving, risk management, regulation, and model governance create persistent need for analysis. Demand for expertise can remain healthy even as automation reduces hours per deliverable and pressures fees.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are professional liability, confidentiality, model error, client concentration, dependence on credentialed principals, carrier insourcing, fee compression, and uncertainty in the residual-code firm population. The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio is unusually high and may reflect timing, classification, or denominator mismatch, so it should not be treated as direct evidence of addressable labor economics.
narrative.principal_driver: AI can compress repeatable analytical and documentation work while recurring regulatory and risk decisions preserve demand for accountable expert service.
narrative.principal_weakness: Productivity gains may reduce billable effort or be competed into fees, and many small practices depend on individual experts whose relationships and credentials are difficult to transfer.
